coredata.dc:title: Enhancing physical properties of polyvinyl alcohol film using carbon quantum dots upcycled from polyethylene terephthalate waste
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Hyunjin
Park, Kitae
Ansari, Jamilur R.
Jang, Jaeyoung
Ahn, Kihyeon
Seo, Jongchul
coredata.dc:description: Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is extensively used in various industries. This study presents an approach for upcycling PET waste by synthesizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using a one-step hydrothermal process. The PET-derived CQDs (PQDs) were incorporated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to develop PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films with enhanced mechanical, thermal, and barrier properties for use as advanced packaging materials. Spherical PQDs with an average diameter of 4 ± 1 nm were successfully synthesized using a hydrothermal method. The incorporation of 1 % PQDs into the PVA matrix resulted in a remarkable enhancement of the oxygen barrier properties, achieving an increase of 99.8 %. Additionally, the Young's modulus of the resulting nanocomposite films improved significantly by 96.3 %. These findings highlight a substantial enhancement in the mechanical and structural integrity of the material. Structural and morphological analyses confirmed that the PQDs had a uniform dispersion and enhanced the crystallinity of the PVA matrix. The oxygen transmission rate of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films drastically decreased from 5.5 ± 0.13 to 0.01 ± 0.0 cm3/(m2·d) when the PQDs content increased from 0 to 0.7 wt%. Notably, this value is suitable for high-performance barrier applications. All the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films had good optical transparency. Our findings demonstrate that the valorization of PET waste into carbon nanomaterials offers a sustainable and effective method for enhancing the physical properties of PVA films with broad usage as environmentally friendly packaging solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PET waste
Carbon quantum dots
Polyvinyl alcohol
Oxygen transmission rate
Mechanical property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films
Properties of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Institute of Planning and Evaluation for Technology in Food Agriculture Forestry and Fisheries
Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korea Institute of Planning and Evaluation for Technology in Food, Agriculture and Forestry
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Institute of Planning and Evaluation for Technology in Food, Agriculture and Forestry
Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The mechanical properties of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films were evaluated by tensile testing. As illustrated in Fig. 10(a) and (b), the mechanical performance of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films was strongly dependent on the PQDs concentration. The pure PVA film exhibited typical ductile behavior with a tensile strength of 7.4 MPa and elongation at break of 289 %. As the PQDs concentration increased to 1 wt%, the tensile strength linearly increased from 47.3 to 59.0 MPa, and the elongation at break decreased from 162.0 % to 86.0 %. This phenomenon may be caused by the strong interactions between the PQDs and PVA chains, resulting in molecular rearrangement and reordering within the amorphous regions of PVA, thereby increasing its crystallinity, as supported by the DSC, XRD, and SEM analyses [41,43,46]. The incorporation of PQDs into PVA-based nanocomposite films significantly enhances their crystallinity, resulting in a denser and more organized structural framework. This structural refinement markedly increases the stiffness of the films while preserving the molecular arrangement. The presence of nanofillers promotes tighter packing and alignment of the polymer chains, thereby bolstering the mechanical integrity and overall performance of the material. These advancements yield a more resilient composite suitable for sophisticated applications [49]. Notably, the uniform distribution of PQDs within the PVA matrix leads to a substantial increase in interfacial binding energy, enhancing Young's modulus by 96.3 % when compared to pure PVA (Table 2). The mechanical properties of these PVA-based nanocomposite films are highly contingent upon the concentration of PQDs, with the optimal formulation demonstrating a maximum tensile strength of 59.19 \u00B1 3.2 MPa at a PQD loading of 1 %. In conclusion, a small amount of PQDs as nanofillers can effectively reinforce the PVA morphology and physical properties [50].The authors would like to express their appreciation to the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) for their financial support through the Brain Pool Fellowship [Grant Number RS-2023-00263418]; a grant funded by the Korean government (MSIP) [Grant Number RS-2023-00208596]; and the Korea Institute of Planning and Evaluation for Technology in Food, Agriculture and Forestry (IPET) through the High Value-added Food Technology Development Program, funded by the Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs (MAFRA) [Grant Number RS-2022-IP322015]. We would like to thank Editage (www.editage.co.kr) for English language editing.

The authors would like to express their appreciation to the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF ) for their financial support through the Brain Pool Fellowship [Grant Number RS-2023-00263418]; a grant funded by the Korean government ( MSIP ) [Grant Number RS-2023-00208596]; and the Korea Institute of Planning and Evaluation for Technology in Food, Agriculture and Forestry ( IPET ) through the High Value-added Food Technology Development Program, funded by the Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs ( MAFRA ) [Grant Number RS-2022-IP322015]. We would like to thank Editage ( www.editage.co.kr ) for English language editing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films.
PL spectra of aqueous PQDs dispersions (a) with different PQDs concentrations and (b) at different excitation wavelengths (λ ex).
(a) TEM image of the PQDs. (b) Diameter distribution of the PQDs. The curve was fitted to the data using a Gaussian model. (c) HR-TEM image of an individual PQDs. (d) Lattice fringes of the PQDs.
FTIR spectra of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films with varying PQDs loadings.
Transmittance of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films.
Photographs of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films.
(a) TGA and (b) DSC curves of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films.
XRD patterns of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films: (a) 2θ = 15°–60°; (b) 2θ = 12°–30°.
(a–f) Top and (g–l) cross-sectional SEM images of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films.
Mechanical properties of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films: (a) stress–strain curves and (b) tensile strength and elongation at break.
Thermal properties of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films.
Mechanical properties and OTR of pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite film.

head.ce:title: Enhancing physical properties of polyvinyl alcohol film using carbon quantum dots upcycled from polyethylene terephthalate waste

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Packaging & Logistics, Yonsei University, 1 Yonseidae-gil, Wonju-si, Gangwon-do, 26493, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Packaging & Logistics, Yonsei University, 1 Yonseidae-gil, Wonju-si, Gangwon-do, 26493, South Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Packaging & Logistics, Yonsei University, 1 Yonseidae-gil, Gangwon-do, 26493, South Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is extensively used in various industries. This study presents an approach for upcycling PET waste by synthesizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using a one-step hydrothermal process. The PET-derived CQDs (PQDs) were incorporated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to develop PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films with enhanced mechanical, thermal, and barrier properties for use as advanced packaging materials. Spherical PQDs with an average diameter of 4 ± 1 nm were successfully synthesized using a hydrothermal method. The incorporation of 1 % PQDs into the PVA matrix resulted in a remarkable enhancement of the oxygen barrier properties, achieving an increase of 99.8 %. Additionally, the Young's modulus of the resulting nanocomposite films improved significantly by 96.3 %. These findings highlight a substantial enhancement in the mechanical and structural integrity of the material. Structural and morphological analyses confirmed that the PQDs had a uniform dispersion and enhanced the crystallinity of the PVA matrix. The oxygen transmission rate of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films drastically decreased from 5.5 ± 0.13 to 0.01 ± 0.0 cm3/(m2·d) when the PQDs content increased from 0 to 0.7 wt%. Notably, this value is suitable for high-performance barrier applications. All the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films had good optical transparency. Our findings demonstrate that the valorization of PET waste into carbon nanomaterials offers a sustainable and effective method for enhancing the physical properties of PVA films with broad usage as environmentally friendly packaging solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is a versatile polymer with excellent physical properties, including high mechanical strength, high thermal and chemical stability, low gas permeability, good affordability, and widespread availability [1,2]. Therefore, it is extensively used in various industries, particularly in food and beverage packaging. It is one of the most widely produced plastics, with an estimated annual production of approximately 60 million tons [3]. Notably, over 60 % of all plastic bottles are produced from PET, highlighting its extensive use as a single-use packaging material [4,5]. However, its widespread use has led to significant environmental challenges, particularly concerning the end-of-life management of PET waste and its accumulation in ecosystems. Because PET is not biodegradable, its degradation can take up to 500 years, during which harmful toxins are released into the environment, posing serious risks to human health and biodiversity [6]. Although recycling efforts are essential to address these issues, the current rate of PET recycling remains alarmingly low, with only 18 % of PET waste being repurposed as new products [7,8].

In response to the aforementioned challenges, the Sustainable Development Goals emphasize the importance of a circular economy for plastic packaging, aiming to reduce the reliance on primary resources and minimize the environmental footprint of packaging waste [9]. A promising approach to address the issue of PET waste is its conversion into value-added products such as carbon nanomaterials. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm, have recently gained significant attention owing to their exceptional properties [10]. CQDs are recognized for their water solubility, cost-effectiveness, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and multifunctionality, including strong antioxidant, antibacterial, and ultraviolet (UV)-blocking capabilities [11,12]. Furthermore, CQDs can be easily synthesized from natural resources such as dried leaves [13], ginkgo leaves [14], and citric acid [15], as well as waste food [16] and plastic [17]. Their unique properties, as well as their natural and environmentally friendly sources, make CQDs suitable candidates for incorporation into biopolymer-based packaging films, offering a new avenue for the development of active packaging materials [18].

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is classified as a “biopolymer”, specifically a synthetic biopolymer resulting from the hydrolysis of polyvinyl acetate. This polymer, derived from ethylene, can be synthesized from renewable resources, enhancing its sustainability profile [19]. PVA is well-regarded for its low cost, nontoxicity, biodegradability, biocompatibility and favorable mechanical properties [20], along with its superior film-forming capabilities. The hydroxyl groups of PVA enable good miscibility and compatibility with other materials, making it an ideal matrix for incorporating nanofillers [21]. However, the high UV transmittance, hydrophilicity, and water solubility of PVA limit its applications in food packaging, requiring improvements in its functional properties. Integrating nanomaterials such as CQDs into PVA has been identified as an effective approach for enhancing the structural and barrier properties, thereby expanding the potential of PVA in active packaging materials [22,23].

Despite the potential of CQDs for enhancing the physical properties of PVA films, research regarding the use of CQDs derived from recycled PET in PVA-based active packaging materials remains limited. This study aims to address this gap by developing a facile hydrothermal method for synthesizing fluorescent CQDs from PET waste as a carbon precursor. The PET-derived CQDs (PQDs) were subsequently incorporated into PVA matrices at various loadings to develop functional packaging films. The physicochemical, barrier, and structural properties of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films were evaluated, and their potential applications in active food packaging were investigated. This study contributes to the development of sustainable and high-performance packaging materials, aligning with global efforts to achieve a circular economy and reduce the environmental impact of plastic waste.

Recycled PET flakes provided by Saehan Platech Co., Ltd. (Cheongju, South Korea) were utilized as carbon precursors for the synthesis of PQDs. Commercial-grade PVA resin (F-17 grade; degree of hydrolysis: 98.0 %–99.5 %; molecular weight: 74,800 g/mol) was sourced from OCI Co., Ltd. (Incheon, South Korea). All other reagents and chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout all experimental procedures.

PQDs were synthesized from recycled PET flakes using a one-step hydrothermal method, as demonstrated in Fig. 1 . Initially, 3 g of PET flakes were dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water with continuous stirring. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor. The reactor was heated to 250 °C and maintained at this temperature for 12 h. The reactor was then allowed to cool to room temperature. The reaction product was subsequently centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 20 min to separate the unreacted material. The supernatant was passed through a 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove any remaining impurities, yielding uniform and small PQDs. The final filtrate containing the PQDs was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C until further use.

PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films composed of PVA and PQDs were prepared using a solution casting method. First, 50 g of PVA was dissolved in 450 mL of distilled water with vigorous stirring at 90 °C for 4 h to obtain a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, the PQDs solution with concentrations (0.1 %, 0.3 %, 0.5 %, 0.7 %, and 1.0 %) w/w relative to 10 wt % of PVA was gradually added to the PVA solution followed by continuous stirring for 1 h to ensure an even dispersion of PQDs. The resulting film-forming solution was cast onto a glass plate (24 cm × 30 cm) coated with Teflon (Cole-Parmer Instrument Co., Ltd., Chicago, IL, USA) and dried at 25 °C for 3 d. Once dried, the nanocomposite film was carefully peeled off the plate and conditioned at 25 °C and 50 % relative humidity for a minimum of 3 d prior to further testing. For comparison, a pure PVA film was prepared following the same procedure but without the incorporation of the PQDs solution. The resulting films are denoted as PVA (0 %), PVA/PQDs (0.1 %), PVA/PQDs (0.3 %), PVA/PQDs (0.5 %), PVA/PQDs (0.7 %), and PVA/PQDs (1.0 %) according to the PQDs solution used.

A fluorescence spectrometer (RF-6000; Shimadzu Corp., Tokyo, Japan) was used to determine the photoluminescence (PL) characteristics of the PQDs. The PL spectra of the PQDs in deionized water were measured in a glass cell at excitation wavelengths of 330–400 nm in 10 nm increments.

The morphology of the PQDs was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-ARM200F NEOARM, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) and high-resolution (HR)-TEM (JEM 2100, JEOL) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The HR-TEM sample was prepared by dropping the PQDs solution onto a 200-mesh carbon-coated copper TEM grid, followed by drying in a vacuum oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films

ce:section.ce:para: Attenuated total reflection Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (65 FTIR, PerkinElmer, Inc., Waltham, MA, USA) was used to determine the chemical structure of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films. Each specimen was analyzed in transmission mode using 64 scans in the 4000–400 cm−1 wavenumber range. Air was used as the reference medium for the analytical process.

Using the diffuse reflectance spectroscopy transmission mode, UV–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometry (UV 2600i, Shimadzu) was used to assess the transmittance of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films in the 200–800 nm wavelength range.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA; TGA-4000, PerkinElmer) of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films was performed from 30 to 800 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC; Q10, TA Instruments, New Castle, DE, USA) was performed from −50 to 250 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. The results were used to analyze the melting and glass transition temperatures, enthalpy of melting, and degree of crystallinity of the as-prepared pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films. The results are given as the mean ± standard deviation of three measurements. Significant differences were determined using Duncan's multiple-range test and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) in SPSS Statistics 29.0 (IBM). Significance was set at p < 0.05.

The pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Smart Lab diffractometer (Ultima IV, Rigaku Corp., Tokyo, Japan) at a wavelength of 0.1539 nm. The results of the film samples were normalized by conducting a background run and correcting the measured XRD intensities by matching the integrated intensity over the 2θ = 58°–60° range.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM; JEOL-7800 F, JEOL) was conducted at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV to analyze the morphology and microstructure of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films. To analyze their cross-sections, the films were frozen in liquid nitrogen and then broken to produce fracture surfaces. Prior to examination, a thin layer of gold was sputtered onto the samples using a sputter-coating apparatus (Q150R-ES; Quorum Technologies, Ltd., Laughton, UK).

The mechanical properties of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films were evaluated by tensile testing using a universal testing machine (QM100T, Qmesys Co., Uiwang, South Korea) equipped with a load cell capable of handling up to 20 kgf and operated at a crosshead speed of 20 mm/s, in accordance with ASTM D638.

The oxygen transmission rate (OTR) of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films was assessed using an OTR 8101e oxygen permeation analyzer (OX-TRAN 702, MOCON Inc., Minneapolis, MN, USA) at a temperature of 23 °C under conditions of 0 % relative humidity, following the ASTM D398 guidelines. The measurement process involved positioning each film in a dedicated cell, followed by introducing an oxygen flow on one side of the film. The OTR was calculated by averaging the observed OTR values, multiplying this average by the thickness of the film, and subsequently dividing it by the oxygen flow rate within the cell of the testing apparatus.

The PL properties of the as-prepared PQDs were systematically investigated to elucidate their fundamental optical characteristics. As depicted in Fig. 2 (a), the excitation and emission spectra clearly confirm the presence of the quantum confinement effect and related photoexcitation phenomena in the PQDs [24]. Because quantum confinement is significant when the size of the PQDs approaches or becomes smaller than the Bohr exciton radius, the electronic states of the PQDs are discretized, leading to a size-dependent shift in both the excitation and emission wavelengths (λ ex and λ em) respectively. The nanoscale dimensions of the PQDs restrict the motion of charge carriers (electrons and holes), thereby increasing the energy band gap and resulting in blue-shifted emission. This effect becomes more pronounced as the particle size decreases, which explains the observed PL behavior. Fig. 2(a) indicates that the as-prepared PQDs have monodisperse and uniform size distributions under aqueous conditions, with excitation and emission peaks at 315 and 415 nm, respectively. The optimal λ ex was 318 nm. As shown in Fig. 2(b), λ em shifted from 415 to 445 nm as λ ex increased to 400 nm, suggesting photon reabsorption and excitation-dependent PL behavior, which is characteristic of quantum confinement in quantum dots [25]. This red-shift highlights the broadening of the absorption spectrum owing to quantum confinement, wherein smaller PQDs absorb higher-energy photons. This is confirmed by the concentration-dependent PL results in Fig. 2(a), which demonstrate that the PL intensity at λ em = 415 nm (λ ex = 310 nm) increased gradually as the PQDs concentration increased from 0.1 to 1 wt%. This is attributed to enhanced photon reabsorption. The energy band gap (E g), as calculated using the Planck equation [26], was 330–400 nm (2.79–2.99 eV), with band gap widening as λ em decreased. This confirms the quantum confinement effect in the PQDs. Furthermore, it underscores the significant role of quantum confinement in determining the optical and electronic properties of these nanomaterials.

The size and morphology of the synthesized PQDs were analyzed using TEM. Fig. 3 (a) indicates that the PQDs comprised particles with different carbon densities, as indicated by the variation in dark contrast among the particles. The PQDs were consistently sized and exhibited a spherical morphology when dispersed in water, as depicted in Fig. 3(a). The synthesized PQDs were less than 10 nm in diameter, with an average size of 4 ± 1 nm (Fig. 3(b)). Moreover, HR-TEM revealed a well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.21 nm for the PQDs, as shown in Fig. 3(c) and (d), which is consistent with the d-spacing of the (100) plane of graphene [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectra of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films with different PQDs contents. The FTIR spectrum of the pure PVA film exhibited several distinctive peaks at 3475 cm−1 (OH), 2933 cm−1 (CH2), 1713 cm−1 (CO), 1658 cm−1 (CC), 1432 cm−1 (CH2), 1076 cm−1 (–CO), and 844 cm−1 (C–C) [28,29]. The FTIR spectra of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films were similar to that of the pure PVA film, regardless of the PQD loading; that is, there were no new peaks or apparent shifts in the characteristic peaks. This phenomenon could suggest that no chemical interactions or bond formation occurred between the PVA matrix and PQDs nanofiller; however, considering the chemical structures and functional groups of the PQDs and PVA, interactions between them are highly likely to have occurred. Nevertheless, the concentration of PQDs in this study was extremely low, and the vibrations of the functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl groups) on the PQDs surface or CO and CC bond stretching may have overlapped with the characteristic PVA peaks, resulting in no apparent changes in the FTIR spectra [30–32].

High optical transparency and UV-blocking ability are necessary characteristics for food packaging films [33]. The UV–vis transmission spectra of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films were analyzed to assess the effect of the PQDs on the transparency and UV-blocking ability. As shown in Fig. 5 , the pure PVA film exhibited almost no light absorption in either the visible (400–800 nm) or UV (200–400 nm) regions. The incorporation of PQDs enhanced the UV-blocking ability of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films at 240 nm, depending on the concentration of PQDs, but did not affect the visible light transmittance. The UV–Vis absorption of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films indicates that the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic sp2 groups is responsible for the formation of a graphite-like carbon structure [34]. PQDs smaller than 10 nm offer effective UV-blocking properties while maintaining high visible light transmittance, ensuring that the material retains its optical transparency despite the addition of these particles [35]. Thus, the PQDs effectively and selectively absorb and block UV radiation.

Fig. 6 shows photographs of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films with varying PQDs loadings. These images confirm that, despite the increase in PQDs concentration, all the films remained optically transparent, with no change in color. The inclusion of PQDs did not reduce the transmission in the visible region because the PQDs (<10 nm) were significantly smaller than the wavelength of visible light, enabling light to pass through the film without interference. Consequently, the addition of PQDs enhanced the UV-blocking properties while maintaining the transparency of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films [36].

Fig. 7 (a) shows the TGA curves of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films. The pure PVA film exhibited a general degradation pattern of pure PVA with three weight loss stages [37,38]. The first stage at 70–160 °C is related to the loss of adsorbed water. The second stage at 260–400 °C corresponds to degradation of the PVA side chains. Finally, the third stage at 400–470 °C is associated with decomposition of the polymer backbone [39]. All the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films exhibited a three-step weight loss pattern, similar to that of the pure PVA film. That is, the addition of PQDs did not change the degradation pattern.

DSC was used to analyze the thermal properties of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films, including the glass transition temperature (T g), melting temperature (T m), and enthalpy of melting (ΔH m). The results are summarized in Table 1 . The pure PVA film had a T g of 77 °C and T m of 218 °C. Adding PQDs did not cause significant changes in T g and T m, indicating that the PQDs did not interfere with the heat-based production of PVA. However, the enthalpy of the pure PVA film increased from 64.1 to 75.6 J/g, and the crystallinity (X c) calculated from the enthalpy increased from 45.8 % to 54.0 %. This increase in enthalpy and crystallinity can be attributed to the PQDs altering the microstructure of the PVA chains [40]. PQDs can affect the microstructure, such as the crystal orientation and crystallinity, via lamellae formation and expansion in the in-plane and out-of-plane directions during crystallization [41–43]. Moreover, the incorporation of small PQDs into the PVA chains and the intermolecular interactions between the PVA chains and PQDs may enhance the local ordering of the polymer and improve the crystallinity [43]. The more compact and ordered microstructure and enhanced crystallinity are expected to improve the mechanical and barrier properties of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films.

XRD was used to investigate the effect of the PQDs on the structure of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films. The XRD data were normalized to minimize the thickness effect [44]. The XRD patterns are shown in Fig. 8 (a) and (b). The pure PVA film exhibited a characteristic diffraction peak at 2θ = 19.5°, indicative of its semicrystalline nature. The introduction of PQDs into the PVA film induced an apparent increase in the diffraction peak intensity at 2θ = 19.3° and a slight shift in the peak position from 19.3° to 19.6°, indicating that the crystallinity and compactness of the structure increased, resulting in a smaller intermolecular distance (d-spacing) between the polymer chains [40,42,45,46]. The PQDs and PVA chains undergo strong interactions, resulting in molecular rearrangement and reordering of the PVA chains. Consequently, the crystallinity of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films is enhanced [45]. This result is consistent with the DSC analysis.

SEM was used to investigate the surface and cross-sectional fracture morphologies of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films. As expected, SEM was not suitable for detecting the PQDs in the PVA matrix owing to their low numbers and extremely small size (less than 10 nm) [47]. As shown in Fig. 9 (a)–(f), all films exhibited a smooth surface, irrespective of the PQDs concentration, indicating that the PQDs did not change the surface morphology and were sufficiently dispersed in the PVA matrix. However, the cross-sectional fracture morphologies, as shown in Fig. 9(g)–(l), changed considerably with only a small addition (0.1–1.0 wt%) of PQDs. The cross-sectional fracture surface of the pure PVA film had a typical laminar structure, indicative of its semicrystalline morphology [48]. However, the incorporation of PQDs into the pure PVA film resulted in a noticeably rougher cross-sectional fracture morphology, suggesting a tendency for localized arrangement or ordering within the amorphous regions of PVA when PQDs are introduced. These localized arrangements may be driven by intra- and intermolecular interactions, leading to increased crystallinity and compactness of the microstructure [45]. This increase in crystallinity should improve the mechanical strength and toughness of the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films.

The OTR was measured to investigate the barrier properties of the pure PVA and PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films. As shown in Table 2, the pure PVA film exhibited an OTR of 5.5 ± 0.13 cm3/(m2·d). Introducing PQDs into the PVA film resulted in a significant decrease in the OTR, reducing it to as low as 0.01 ± 0.0 cm3/(m2·d) at a PQDs concentration of 0.7 wt%. The gas permeation in a polymer matrix depends not only on the chemical structure but also on the morphology, such as the crystallinity, orientation, and compactness of the polymer chains. As demonstrated by the DSC, XRD, and tensile testing results, the PQDs enhanced the crystallinity and compactness of the microstructure in the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films. Specifically, the enhanced structural integrity effectively reduced the diffusion pathways for oxygen molecules, resulting in an extremely low OTR [51].

In this study, PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films were developed by incorporating CQDs synthesized from recycled PET flakes into PVA films to enhance their physical and barrier properties. PQDs were successfully synthesized from waste PET flakes by a one-step hydrothermal process. The as-prepared PQDs had a spherical structure with an average particle size of 4 ± 1 nm and lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. The PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films were fabricated using a solution casting method, and their physical properties were systematically evaluated as a function of the PQD's content. The incorporation of PQDs into the pure PVA film resulted in a noticeable increase in the crystallinity, compactness, and order of the microstructure in the PVA/PQDs nanocomposite films. Furthermore, the PVA/PQDs (1 %) matrix resulted in a remarkable enhancement of oxygen barrier properties, achieving a notable reduction of 99.8 %. Additionally, Young's modulus of the resulting nanocomposite films demonstrated a substantial increase of 96.3 %. These findings highlight a significant improvement in both the mechanical characteristics and structural integrity of the material. Thus, we conclude that the sustainable integration of PQDs synthesized from PET waste can improve and control the functional properties of PVA films, making them suitable for advanced packaging applications.

Hyunjin Kim: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Kitae Park: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Jamilur R. Ansari: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Visualization. Jaeyoung Jang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Kihyeon Ahn: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Jongchul Seo: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their appreciation to the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) for their financial support through the Brain Pool Fellowship [Grant Number RS-2023-00263418]; a grant funded by the Korean government (MSIP) [Grant Number RS-2023-00208596]; and the Korea Institute of Planning and Evaluation for Technology in Food, Agriculture and Forestry (IPET) through the High Value-added Food Technology Development Program, funded by the Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs (MAFRA) [Grant Number RS-2022-IP322015]. We would like to thank Editage (www.editage.co.kr) for English language editing.
